speaker: interviewer
text: Renate Rossmere is being interviewed. Mrs. Rossmere, was it difficult for you to leave your home? Or were you no longer needed in your home for you to go out and-- and get this training, or how did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was delighted to leave finally and, and start, start my life in a way. Oh, yes, you're just reminding me. There had been talk of sending me to a residential school to be trained, and with children. And I was very much looking forward to this. And my parents had written away for brochures. And the day before I was supposed to leave, my parents decided that they couldn't afford it and that I had to stay at Corringway after all, and was not, could not do this, to me, wonderful seeming opportunity of getting on with my life. And I was back home. And it was hard because my cousin, my brother, and my other cousin all went to school regularly. My cousins were evacuated, one cousin was evacuated, because of the bombing, with his school, and my older cousin. And it was always wonderful to have her come home from-- from school and-- and bring along tales of what she had learned in school and the books they had read and for me to get a little glimpse of education, which I really craved very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was quite a disappointment, not being able to go to this residential school. But I continued. And I was in touch with my friends, but I had friends. But there was not very much socialization. And of course there was a great deal of-- of bombing. And life just wasn't natural, wasn't the same because you had the bombs flying during the day and at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a matter of fact, in order for us to be-- feel safer, we almost, all of us, except my grandfather, gathered in the living room downstairs and slept on mattresses there. And it was my job every evening to secure the glass door to the outside to the garden with mattresses and ropes to shield us from possible flying glass. I remember that job was a tedious job to do. Then in the morning, take it all down again. But there we slept for us for many months. And when the bombing got too bad, we all got up and gathered together in the kitchen and had something to eat, just as a relief, because it was a very nerve-wracking time. And of course, we all craved sugar and we were very much rationed. And everybody had their own pot of jam, one pound a month or something like that. Mine was always gone the first, because I seemed to crave this sugar more than anybody else, so-- so then I had to go to others and ask them for a little bit of jam. That was just a little incident. Luckily, our house was not bombed. We were living near a lovely piece of land called Hampstead Heath, where the big guns were. And we kind of-- whenever they went off, we knew they were doing their bit and shooting trying to shoot down the enemy. And that was quite-- I felt quite protective in spite of the enormous noise they made. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your adolescence?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my adolescence, oh yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no dating, there were no parties.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a little bit of dating because of my future husband. And his mother also had got out of her Hamburg, and his father. And at one point, his mother lived with us, and he came to visit his mother. And so we-- and we had written to each other and became friends. And so he really took me out on my first date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the same age as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two years older. And we went to a Chinese-- we went to a movie. I think it was It was Vivien Leigh and Robert Taylor, I think. And afterwards, we went to a Chinese restaurant. I'd never been to a Chinese restaurant. It was all very lovely. And after that, we started a friendship, which really continued for six years. My husband was also interned on the Isle of Man. And in order to get his release, he had to volunteer to join the army, the British Army. That was one way he got out. His brother was also on the Isle of-- was also interned, I'm not sure where. And he was sent to Canada on a, on a-- with refugees. Another friend was sent to Australia. But my husband joined the army. And the Pioneer Corps was all you were allowed to join at that time. And later on, he was able to be in the Tank Corps. He was radio operator, I think, in a tank, and was allowed to then be shipped to the continent. He had to change his name from Rosenmeyer to Rossmere and from Andreas to Andrew In order-- in case-- he became captured by the Nazis, and so that they wouldn't recognize him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we kept up this friendship for six years. It was writing lots of letters. He was sent-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he joined the Scottish Orchestra in the army because he played the flute. He was very musical. And so they went from one place in Scotland to another. And he always sent postcards from each day to his mother and to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have all those postcards and I took a trip a few years back back to Scotland together with the postcards and went to all the places where he had been to visit them and just to-- to see what they were like. And that was very exciting to do that. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the war, we got married in 1948, in August 1948. And when did you have the opportunity to go to-- learned how to be a nursery school teacher to deal with these children? You said you then were eventually sent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I was 20, I was sent, I was allowed to leave the house and get my training in this residential place in Camden Town. And it was a lovely time, and was trained, went not only practically, but also academically. I was, we attended some school or other and were trained, totally trained, in the-- it was more than a teacher, it was also taking care of the physical needs of children. Also, we were taught some psychology. It was a fairly rounded program, after which I could then apply as a teacher and worked for a few years as a teacher in schools in London, nursery teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever talk of evacuating you and your brother and cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my cousin, one cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was evacuated with her school.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not you-- not you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father ever able to find work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He started an import/export business, a very small one, and it wasn't too successful. It was a lot of hard work and a lot of unfortunate breaks he had, so I don't think he was too successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother go out to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she stayed at home and looked after the household, so, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't too strong. My aunt went to work. My mother wasn't too strong. So she mainly, I remember her doing the marketing, leaving with her little basket every morning, walking of course to the shopping center, which was about 15 minutes away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day the war was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at the Camden Town Nursery, day nursery. And it was a wonderful day. We stood on the roofs of Camden Town and I watched all the parades from the roof. And then later on, we went to Piccadilly Circus and joined the crowds there and the singing. And it was a wonderful day. Great relief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you an angry teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all, no, very placid and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any anger towards Germany, towards--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. And my parent's-- my father's relatives all died in concentration camps, both his sisters and their families died in concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ones, which-- which camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know which camps. One family lived in Berlin. I don't know where they were taken to. It was his sister and two children, their two, her two daughters. They were slightly older than I was. And the other family lived in Amsterdam and was a together family. And their-- they-- they were taken because apparently the husband of my father's sister said, well, they will never touch me, although he had friends in America and he never made use of that because he was so sure he would never be the one to be murdered. He went with his one daughter, Ruth. And his second daughter hid at her boyfriend's house, who was not Jewish, and was saved. Her life was saved and I'm still in contact with her. She still lives in Amsterdam now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your feeling today about Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a beautiful country, but I would prefer not ever to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't mind visiting it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't mind it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very strange feeling. I went back the first time with my husband because he wanted to celebrate his 50th birthday there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something unfulfilled, something that he needed to do. He needed to stay in one of the biggest and fanciest hotels, just because that appealed to him at that point to do that. He had always heard of these hotels and he was curious what it was really like. We had a lovely time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened when you, were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not frightened, but apprehensive. And when we arrived at the airport in Hamburg, we took a taxi. And it was a woman driver, taxi driver. And we told her to stop at various places and went and told her to stop at our homes where we had lived, and our grandparents homes, et cetera, et cetera. And she did. It took us a long time to get from the airport to the hotel, but we finally did. And as I was getting out of the car, she bent down and she produced a bouquet of flowers and she gave it to me. And she said, I too, knew what it means to come back. And with that, she handed me the flowers. It was very moving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll never forget that. And on the other hand, we had some instances where people in shops said, well, it wasn't our fault and you can't blame us. It was pretty devastating to hear that. But on the whole, it was a good experience to come back and see it, see it as a town, because it was pretty damaged during the war. And the other thing is that my husband was in the army. And he was sent to Hamburg and the tank. And he was the one, the first one, because he knew the way, to enter Hamburg on the tank as a British, capturing the town of Hamburg. That must have been a pretty exciting moment for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when did you-- when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in August '48 in London in a registry, a very tiny little wedding. Nobody had anything after the war. But we had a very delicious lunch, which my mother-in-law cooked, then a reception afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide to come to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really my husband's work. He had worked for the-- he had started work with an insurance broker in Hamburg and had the same job with the same firm in London. And they had a branch, a big branch here in New York and other branches too in the country, and they offered him a job. And he jumped to it because he felt all the time America was the land for him to live his life and to prosper. And he felt in England that was not, he would not have been able to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was disappointed. I loved England. I was-- I liked the people, I liked everything there. I did not want to come to America. At the time, I was in-- very shortly after our marriage, I was accepted in a one year's teachers college course, an emergency course, four people, after the war because teachers were so-- so much in demand and there were so few left. And I was in middle of this when I received a postcard from my husband-- we are leaving in a month or so for-- for America. And I had to again, abandon, my education. You know, this was the third time I had done this. And I decided I wanted to follow and go with him to America. That was more important to me than my education. But it never seemed to have worked out. Anyway, we left then in April 1949 to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children were born here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born here. Yes, they're Americans. And we became American, became naturalized. We had become naturalized in England. My husband through the army and I had done this separately. Oh, I wanted to tell you about the painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, well, we're going to see that painting.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, and then you want me to tell you the story then?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us please about what happened to you here. Did you-- did you feel that you belonged in a short order, or were you look on as a refugee?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never here. In England, yes-- but never here. In England, that is something you could not get away from. And I believe you never can. That's part of England, and you accepted it But here, no. You were immediately one of everybody, and that was a very good feeling, indeed. And that's what my husband really liked about coming to American, having-- being able to do what you want and having the chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know that you were able to fulfill the dream once you came here. Would you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, about my work?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your work, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started out here when my son was three and 1/2, I decided he-- it was a good idea to have him join a nursery class-- nursery school class. And I was lucky knowing some friends who were affiliated with the Windward School in White Plains, a private school. And I inquired about it and I liked what they stood for, so I enrolled my son. And at the same time, applied as a teacher, as an assistant teacher's job there, and was hired by Dr. [INAUDIBLE], whom you know also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: As an assistant in the nursery. So I had the-- David and I had the same hours and the same holidays, the same vacations, and that was wonderful. It worked out very well. And he stayed there for two years through kindergarten, and I stayed for 10 years. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there I took a sabbatical. I tried to take a sabbatical, which only turned out a few months. And I was then called-- I had met a lot of lovely teachers. And they were all almost all refugees also. And they went on to Sarah Lawrence and other places to work as nursery teachers. And one of them was Doris [? Goren, ?] and she became the founder. She and Dr. Kliman became the founders of the Center for Preventive Psychology in White Plains. And when I-- starting out very small with just three or four children and a teacher, Doris was a teacher and an assistant. And Dr. Kliman is the psychiatrist. It was a new way to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during my sabbatical I was called by Doris, we need somebody to-- somebody left and we need somebody to help us in the nursery. Would you be able to do this? I thought about it and I felt it was a good opportunity to start in a new place and I agreed and have been there ever since. How long have you been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came, I joined there in 1968. And it is now 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: A long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever come over to this country, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents came to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to live, no. They stayed in London and 1 Corringway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now so many years later, 50-plus years later, when you look back at your experiences, how do you feel that they've shaped your life? Shaped your-- not just, not just the facts of your life but the feelings of your life, your-- the decisions you've made, the kind of woman you have become?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that it's been a good experience. I feel very strongly that if I had stayed in Germany in Hamburg, I would have been a spoiled brat, not a very good person, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very immature person. And I think by fleeing to London and going through the experiences I had gone through and looking after my family, it has shaped my life tremendously. And I feel that, due to that, I've become a much better person than I would ever been without that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you be a spoiled brat in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a well-to-do family. I-- I had no responsibilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not called--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a very immature person, really, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and not enjoyed the schooling very much and had no real idea of what I wanted to do. But then, who does at 14, you know? Still, I really feel that very strongly, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Has this influenced your children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have, yes, because I've become a better person, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know in any specific ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, with all being-- as a religion, you talked about religion before. We've all joined-- Andrew and I joined the Ethical Culture and attended regularly. And the children attended regularly, too. While my son David has not kept this up, Jennifer my daughter has and has become active in Washington and Virginia in Ethical Culture society. And her husband also is active in Washington society, so I feel it's-- it's done something. We did try at one point to-- for Jennifer to join a synagogue and take religious instruction there. And we weren't too pleased with that. It just didn't feel natural. We tried to celebrate Hanukkah, tried to keep the holidays, and it just wasn't comfortable. And so we decided to not go along with this and then joined Ethical after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean today for you to be Jewish, to have suffered so?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means to be very proud of it. From a point-of-view of heritage, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I've been to Israel. I-- I think it's just wonderful what has been done. And you know, I wish sometimes that we would have had a background, that we would have had a religious background and we would have been-- we would have celebrated the holidays and somehow maybe even be closer than we were. But I don't know. But it would give you some-- some solidity, which I never had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you making this tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like my children and grandchildren to hear my story one day, if they're so interested. And that's the reason I decided to go along with it and have it done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other thoughts that you would like to speak about now in reflecting back over your history?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hoping and wishing that my family can have an easier life than I did and more chances, which I know educationally they've had. And I'm very grateful for that and that my grandchildren will equally have the same opportunities, and hopefully take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, you're welcome.
